## Site of the Day - Dec 17, 2013

# [FlatGuitars](http://www.flatguitars.com/)

- [![](https://assets.awwwards.com/awards/media/cache/thumb_user_70/avatar/17420/5a956d097e9de.jpg)**David Navarro**](https://www.awwwards.com/davidnavarro/)

- [![](https://assets.awwwards.com/awards/media/cache/thumb_user_70/avatar/79469/677049be39d7b582107402.png)**Ingamana**](https://www.awwwards.com/ingamana/)


- ![](https://assets.awwwards.com/awards/images/2013/12/flat-guitars-website.png)

## Description

### FlatGuitars is an illustration tribute to historic guitars and the guitar heroes behind them. Handcrafted with love under the effect of rock'n'roll by David Navarro & Ingamana.

web.description: ... shop. However, the summer heat was brutal, so I moved all my ... Guitar Repair - Why Guitar Repair is in Danger of Becoming a Lost Art.
web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/clJt0BwV-xs/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [Luthier Life: Workshop Upgrade & Inspiration](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=clJt0BwV-xs)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Mark Gutierrez](https://www.youtube.com/@MarkGutierrez)
**Uploaded at**: 2024-06-28
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 04:59
**Views**: 1168
**Likes**: 79
**Category**: Howto & Style

## Description

```
I finally got around to adding insulation and drywall to the shop. It was built sometime last year, and I moved in immediately without finishing it. Winter and spring were lovely here in Texas, and I enjoyed spending time in the shop. However, the summer heat was brutal, so I moved all my tools out and hired a crew to finish it up. They installed foam insulation in the ceiling and RC15 in the walls. The drywall was painted a very bright white, and it looks fantastic.

Recently, my neighbor's house caught on fire. I woke up at 5 a.m. to the sound of yelling outside. When I went to the backyard to investigate, I saw smoke and flames. Fortunately, no one was in the burning house, and everyone is fine. The shop is also unharmed.

Merch
My Guitar Blueprints, DXFs, Fusion Files: https://who-wants-honey.myshopify.com/
My Shirt: https://who-wants-honey.creator-spring.com/listing/new-labyrinthine
Let's be friends: https://www.buymeacoffee.com/markgutierrez

All Music Composed by Mark Gutierrez
```

web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (640x480)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/Loh76JKUsJU/sddefault.jpg?sqp=-oaymwEmCIAFEOAD8quKqQMa8AEB-AHUBoAC4AOKAgwIABABGGUgYCg_MA8=&rs=AOn4CLAaSrre_ne0u_wc20klN26jXJAaxg)
# [Luthier Tips du Jour Mailbag  29 - Shop design](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Loh76JKUsJU)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [OBrienGuitars](https://www.youtube.com/@OBrienGuitars)
**Uploaded at**: 2015-03-09
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 14:30
**Views**: 16195
**Likes**: 281
**Category**: Education

## Description

```
Luthier and Instructor, Robert O'Brien offers advice on shop layout and design and also gives some tips about shop efficiently that he has learned. https://obrienguitars.com‍

🔸 Shop favorites
✦ Mask
› RZ Mask: https://amzn.to/380WuQo 

✦ Saw blades
Thin kerf saw blade for cutting Spanish heel slots and bracing:
› I use this blade: https://amzn.to/357XgJc
› Freud has a similar blade that I have also used: https://amzn.to/3b5fWgr
› You can even get them in a 5 pack: https://amzn.to/3ociE7E

✦ Sanding and Scraping
› Micromesh: https://amzn.to/3olE4iF
› Scraper e burnisher: https://amzn.to/3pKRNzY

✦ Dust collectors
› Oneida high pressure system (14 gallon): https://amzn.to/380dYwf 
› Oneida Deluxe Dust Deputy (10 gallon): https://amzn.to/3n0YkVp 
› Oneida Deluxe Dust Deputy (5 gallon): https://amzn.to/2Le90TG 
› Oneida cyclone separator only:  https://amzn.to/3hv5EHG
› Oneida Mini-Gorilla Portable Cyclone Dust Collector: https://amzn.to/2WW2tzq 
› Jet cyclone cust collector: https://amzn.to/2MkGP5Y 
› Jet collector only: https://amzn.to/34YTDFq  
› Powermatic dust collector - https://amzn.to/380GD40

✦ Air filtration
› Powermatic Air Filtration: https://amzn.to/3pBP7ol 
› Jet Air Filtration: https://amzn.to/3pCpYdi 

🔸 Brazilian treats
If you need some Brazilian coffee here are a couple of brands I purchase when I go to Brazil. They are also available through Amazon:
› Melitta: https://amzn.to/3o7eQEE
› Pilão: https://amzn.to/3n3p5Zs
Cheese bread:
› Box of 8  - https://amzn.to/353kJeG
› Brazibites: https://amzn.to/2JDA26p

This video and description contains affiliate links. If you click on a link, I may receive a commission. Money earned helps to support my channel.
```

## Transcript

[Music]
today's tips to your mailbag question
comes to us from Wisconsin dear Robert I
have never seen you do a video about
your shop I am beginning to design my
shop and would like to hear your input
thank you for all your videos Peter in
Wisconsin Peter this is one topic I've
learned a lot about over the years not
only my own shops I've had several of
them over the years but I've also
visited and been in countless shops of
other luers from around the world and I
think I have a good idea of what works
best for me now I'm going to put some of
this information out there in no
particular order and perhaps some of
this will be beneficial to you let's
start with space It's never enough if
you have a 50qt shop or a 50,000 ft shop
you will always fill it up that's just
the way it is I have learned that the
smaller the shop I work in the more
efficient I have to be working in that
shop and that could be a benefit
with that in mind let's talk about tools
your style of work whether you work with
more power tools or more hand tools is
going to determine the amount of space
you need in your shop now I use a
combination I'm more of a hand tool guy
but I have all the big tools and stuff
too to kind of speed the flow up if I
need to when I was working in a smaller
shop one of the things I learned is to
put everything on mobile bases all of my
tools even my shop today I have a much
larger shop but all of my tools are
still on casters when I worked in a
small shop what I used to do is have a
what I call a tool parking lot one
little corner of my shop all of my tools
were parked and there sometimes two and
three deep and when I needed one I'd
call The Violet guy and he we'd pull all
those tools out and we'd use one uh and
then put it back in there that worked to
my benefit as well because all of the
Dust was confined to one small area of
the shop I didn't need a large dust
collector system I just had one small
vacuum I'd wheel the tool over to it
plug it in when I was done I'd wheel the
tool back into the parking l
so it's not ideal but it worked but to
this day all of my tools even some of my
benches are still on Caster some of the
tools that you choose to use in your
shop you think may be a necessity
however some of them are luxury items
for example my dual drum sandard I
worked for years with a safety planer
scrapers and hand planes to thickness my
lumber now the thickness sandard is
definitely a luxury item it speeds
things up but you don't have to have it
people built guitars and still build
guitars without those larger tools like
large table saws band saws things that
you use perhaps only once in a while
think about it before taking up a large
part of the real estate of the shop with
those larger tools also table saws and
other benches have a tendency to collect
junk the more benches you have in your
shop the more junk you're going to put
on them if you limit your bench space
perhaps you can help keep your shop a
little cleaner storage is also another
problem you got to have space to store
things your tools for example now wall
space is is great if you have it and try
and maximize your wall space that's not
always possible if you have Windows and
things like that but try and maximize
the amount of wall space you have if you
don't have it which I don't have a lot
of wall space in my current shop what I
use is drawers under my benches now the
drawers under the benches allow me to
store a large quantity of tools and
supplies without having to take up a lot
of wall space or bench space so it's
dead space basically under your bench
and that's where I store a lot of my
things I've made a bunch of drawers and
things like that and that works for me
speaking of benches I have several in my
shop but I do a lot of teaching in my
shop I have a lot of people in and out
so I need a little bit of bench space
for people to work perhaps one main
bench is all you're going to need in
your shop make it a sturdy bench and one
that's useful one that has vices ways to
hold things bench dogs things like that
so you can it's useful for you to hold
things and work on it be careful of the
height of the bench if you're always
leaning over to work on your bench it
can cause undo stress and muscle pain so
stand up find a bench that's a little
taller I built my bench personally for
me I'm a tall guy so I made my bench a
little higher and it works for me one
thing I learned the hard way is never
put cabinets over your bench I had a row
of cabinets over my bench where I worked
one time and I had a nice high-end
guitar there on the bench and all it
took was one time for me to open the
cabinet to reach in and get a tool and
that tool jumped out onto my guitar and
you know the result there so I had to do
a little bit of repair work so I don't
recommend cabinets over your bench space
that's another reason why I put drawers
under my benches now and all the tools
are under the drawers and I don't have
that problem let's talk about lighting
lighting is very important you got to be
able to see what you're doing and the
older you get the harder it is to see
things without proper lighting daylight
natural daylight is the best now if you
don't have that option I don't have that
option here in my shop I'm a below grade
in my shop and so I have a couple of
small windows and that's it so I'm
locked in into artificial lighting when
I go with the artificial lighting I go
with the daylight Deluxe fluorescent
bulbs I want the most amount of light
that I can get in my shop that imitates
the natural daylight now you can get the
cool fluorescent lighting or other types
of lightings it's just not enough in my
opinion I like it bright so when you
design your shop make sure that you have
enough Lighting in there so there's no
shadows in some areas and you can see
what you're doing with the light that
you have let's talk about dust control
it's so important that I recently wrote
an article for the Guild of american3
magazine on the subject there are many
many types of dust control systems out
there the main thing you need to decide
is where to put that sucker and to get
the best efficiency out of it how are
you going to run your duct work now if
you don't like listening to the noise of
the machine you might consider putting
it in an out building or outside your
shop and then piping in your duct work
if you don't have that option due to
environmental reasons or HOAs or
neighborhoods things like that you have
to put it on the inside and that's what
I've done in my shop I have a nice unit
in the corner there what I've done is
I've run about 15 or 20 ft of piping to
the left and about 15 or 20 ft of piping
to the right and I have blast Gates
where I can shut off the right side or
shut off the left side and that improves
the efficiency of my system I only run
one side at a time if you try and run
both sides at once then you're losing
CFM in the efficiency of your machine
keep the filters clean as well now I
also have a few shop backs in my shop um
I talk about this all on the Guild of
American liery magazine article I highly
recommend shop backs um I use the the
bigger dust control for my tools and
machinery and then for the floors and
things I use uh the shop packs and those
work very well so make sure that you
study dust control before you design
your shop it's very important something
else that you need to control is your
relative humidity or your Rh levels in
your shop and I've talked about this
before in a couple videos behind me here
I have a climate controlled room and in
there it stays a pretty constant 45% I
also have a goar deck in that room if
I'm building a guitar for a high
relative humidity environment then I can
actually glue the guitar together build
it in the the climate controll room now
if you don't have that luxury then store
your materials in that climate
controlled environment bring them out
work them on your bench and periodically
through the day put them back into that
controlled environment and that will
help that's what I had to do in Brazil
There Was 80 to 100% humidity all the
time I had a little portion of my studio
apartment that I had bumped down with a
dehumidifier to 50% and I would put my
lumber in there before I started working
I'd bring it out and work on it put it
back in there periodically throughout
the day or before glue UPS to make sure
it's dry so relative humidity is very
very important I've mentioned this in
some of my videos before if you're
starting your shop from scratch in other
words you're design it you're going to
pour slab and things like that think
about controlling the moisture content
coming up through the slab whether you
seal it from the inside with epoy or if
you're talking to your contractor or
somebody else who's going to pour the
slab for you make sure they put a
moisture barer or something underneath
it to keep that moisture from seeping up
into the concrete cuz it will happen
also if you have concrete floors your
feet are going to get tired throughout
the day especially if you spend 8 to 10
hours a day in your shopping like I do
so you might want to think about some
type of anti fatigue mats in your shops
however I found it's a little harder to
keep the shop clean if you have a bunch
of anti- fatigue mats all around your
shop another item that I like to have in
my shop is water not only for drinking
but for washing and I have a little
utility sink in my shop here that is
very useful especially for things like
sharpening I need water stones that's
the way I sharpen my tools so I have
water for washing my hands cleaning up
afterwards uh water is very important so
if you have a a water source in your
shop it's very useful another thing that
I have in my shop is a small mini fridge
now I know what you guys are thinking
that's for the beer well occasionally
yes some beer goes in there but beer and
power tools do not mix so be careful but
I like storing things like glue my LMI
white glue my CA glue all of that stuff
is stored inside there so a small mini
fridge is is a good thing if you have
the space now you may not have the space
then you don't need it some place to sit
down every now and then is also
important now I have a bunch of shop
stools around my place here because I
have students coming in all the time now
occasionally those things get in the way
when I'm working by myself so you need
to decide how many stools you need in
your shop if it's just one for yourself
that you're the only person that's going
to be in there if you're going to have
visitors occasionally you need to have
places for them to sit also if you have
people coming in and out periodically to
to try out your instruments to sample
your instruments you need a place for
them to do that so think about that
where are you going to store your stool
when you're not working with it get it
out of the way when you need it you want
it close at hand workflow is also very
important you don't want your tools
scattered all over your shop you don't
want to be walking 20 ft that way then
walking another 20 ft that way to pick
up the tools you need to do the job in
the middle of the shop on your bench
you're going to be very tired at the end
of the day so work efficiently in your
shop put your hand tools your bench
Tools around your bench I've seen guys
that have them on the other side of the
the shop and it just doesn't make sense
to me to walk all the way across there
and come back to your bench you do that
50 times a day you're going to fill it
at the end of the day so work
efficiently store your tools where
you're going to need them cleansing shop
cleaniness is also very important uh not
only do I think it affects tone but it's
also an impression on your work if you
have visitors visiting your shop and
it's all the time cluttered and dirty
then your work the impression that they
have of your work perhaps will be
cluttered and dirty so keep your shop
clean if you need compressed air in your
shop uh think about where you're going
to put that compressor think about how
large the compressor needs to be think
about the noise that it's going to cause
in the shop now I've got mine up in my
garage and I piped the line in and that
works very well because I don't have to
listen to the compressor it also can be
a bad thing too because in my shop I
have vacuum Hold Clamps and I use a
ventu valve hooked up to my compressor
in the shop and it's designed so I don't
have to listen to the hissing of the
Venturi valve but sometimes I forget to
turn it off and I'll go watch TV or have
dinner at the end of the day go to sleep
at night and I hear my compressor and my
garage kicking on because I forgot to
turn off the Venturi valve so think
about something like that now my Venturi
valve uh I've got positive and negative
pressure on the same line and I'll I'll
show you how that works the way my
ventur valve works is I come in from the
garage with a PVC line and that's my
positive pressure I te off of that and
that goes over to my sanding station so
I have positive blowing pressure on the
over by the sanding station then I have
a switch that I can flip and the air
will continue and I put it in a quar
inch clear line and take it back out to
the compressor where the Venturi valve
is hooked onto that line that line then
comes back into my shop and as long as
that valve or that switch is on I have
positive and negative pressure in other
words I have a vacuum sucking on my hold
down clamps and and I have positive
blowing pressure on the same line so
that works pretty efficiently too and
it's outside where I don't have to
listen to it when it comes to finish
work you need to decide what kind of
finish you're going to do and how you're
going to apply it if it's just French
polish you can do it right in your
current shop you can even do it on your
kitchen table if you're doing any amount
of spraying you need to think about that
am I going to install a spray booth am I
going to go to the time and expense of
doing that am I going to take up
valuable real estate in my shop for a
spray booth so think about that as well
Peter something else is very important
to put in your shop or to think about
when designing your shop is fire safety
have at least a couple of fire
extinguishers hanging around we're
working with some very flammable
products sometimes even wood dust can be
very flammable so you don't need a fire
in your shop make sure that you have a
fire extinguisher in your shop and that
it's accessible I have mine on the wall
near the door to my shop so if I have
I'm on the way out in the dead run
there's the fire extinguisher one of the
most important Tools in my shop is the
guitar Builder can of whoop ass now this
tool has many applications throughout
the guitar bending process and comes in
extremely handy and yes it has saved my
hide more than once sometimes just
placing this on the bench next to the
work makes the work go smoother so I
highly recommend guitar Builders kind of
whoop ass and if you are a dog lover
having a shop dog sometimes just helps
the stress melt away but be careful
though don't trip over the dog
especially if you're carrying Sharp
Tools also if you have a a high-end
instrument you're doing some repair work
for a client or one of your own if you
trip over the dog yeah it could be a bad
thing I've heard about that happening so
be careful there if you have a shop Dog
Shop dogs are great but you need to be
careful also be careful if you have
visitors coming into your shop not all
visitors are dog lovers and sometimes
that can be a turnoff to some clients so
make sure that anybody visiting the shop
is dog friendly so Peter thank you very
much for your question this is a topic
I've been wanting to address for a long
time however I could probably write a
book on this subject there's so much
information you need to think about I'm
just beginning to skim the surface here
in this small video so I hope it's been
helpful if you have any questions feel
free to contact me also there's a lot of
websites out there that offer great
insight into shop layout so thank you
very much Peter and happy building
[Music]

web.title: YOUR LUTHIER SHOWCASE - Michael Watts
web.description: If you're looking for a skilled musician, videographer, and storyteller to showcase your instruments, look no further than Michael Watts. He's simply the best!
web.markdown:
- [http://www.youtube.com/user/michaelwattsguitar](http://www.youtube.com/user/michaelwattsguitar "http://www.youtube.com/user/michaelwattsguitar")
- [http://itunes.com/https://itunes.apple.com/gb/album/vetiver/1376062076](http://itunes.com/https://itunes.apple.com/gb/album/vetiver/1376062076 "http://itunes.com/https://itunes.apple.com/gb/album/vetiver/1376062076")
- [https://www.instagram.com/michael.watts.guitar/](https://www.instagram.com/michael.watts.guitar/ "https://www.instagram.com/michael.watts.guitar/")
- [https://open.spotify.com/album/4tf5kEzB0zghV2LEsYfYZy](https://open.spotify.com/album/4tf5kEzB0zghV2LEsYfYZy "https://open.spotify.com/album/4tf5kEzB0zghV2LEsYfYZy")

## YOUR LUTHIER SHOWCASE

![](https://images.zoogletools.com/s:bzglfiles/u/9846/90041b4e8294cb0b1a57521243f93cfab7793a8b/original/img-8954.jpg/!!/b%3AW1siZXh0cmFjdCIseyJsZWZ0Ijo0NTQsInRvcCI6NDUzLCJ3aWR0aCI6NTQyMCwiaGVpZ2h0IjozMDQ4fV0sWyJyZXNpemUiLDEwMDBdLFsibWF4Il0sWyJ3ZSJdXQ%3D%3D/meta%3AeyJzcmNCdWNrZXQiOiJiemdsZmlsZXMifQ%3D%3D.jpg)

## DOES THIS SOUND FAMILIAR?

### Are you looking for someone  to showcase your work at a world class level? Someone who  understands the international guitar making scene and its history, and can contextualise your work within it?

### Maybe you're looking to get your instruments  into the hands of an award-winning musician who can speak eloquently about each guitar and highlight the unique strengths, nuances and beauty of your work?

### You might be looking to get some mentoring or feedback on your work from a player's point of view too - looking at the sonics, ergonomics and aesthetics of the guitars to the moment your client opens the case.

### Are you looking for someone who can make your guitars look and sound as good as possible while sharing the story of you and and your work with enthusiasm to an appreciative audience?

### That's not too much to ask for is it?

![](https://images.zoogletools.com/s:bzglfiles/u/9846/4843e393e7c1952a365d08171ef780769dd69eb2/original/screenshot-2022-01-19-at-17-06-37.png/!!/b%3AW1sicmVzaXplIiwxMDAwXSxbIm1heCJdLFsid2UiXV0%3D/meta%3AeyJzcmNCdWNrZXQiOiJiemdsZmlsZXMifQ%3D%3D.png)

## TESTIMONIALS

> “As a luthier, I sought out Michael Watts to help capture the unique story, sonic palette, and beauty of my latest guitar. My goal was to share this creation with both my past and future clientele, while also reaching Michael’s expansive and respected following.
>
> Working with Michael was a key decision for me because of his reputation within the high-end luthier community and beyond.
>
> From the very first conversation to the completion of the video, Michael has been an absolute pleasure to work with. His ability to bring the most out of a guitar—through his choice of music, playing style, and his overall approach to the project—is exceptional. He has a true gift for showcasing a guitar’s sonic qualities with remarkable precision, and the resulting video has far exceeded my expectations.
>
> The quality of Michael's photography and video production is some of the finest I’ve seen. His attention to detail and the way he highlights the guitar’s aesthetics are unmatched. Beyond his technical skills, Michael brought fresh ideas and a creative vision to the table that I truly appreciated.
>
> His rates reflect the outstanding service he provides, and I can confidently say that working with him has been an incredible experience. I highly recommend him to any luthier looking to showcase their work through high-quality video and photography, and I will certainly continue to collaborate with him in the future.”

—
[RUEBEN FORSLAND - JOI GUITARS](https://www.joiguitars.com/)

> “Michael Watts has contributed untold value to our business and creative endeavours and selflessly been there at every turn with expert advice, inspiring ideas and rock solid dependability. His talent as a musician and depth of knowledge on all things guitar related has made him a completely indispensable ally in the trenches of building a business and reputation as luthiers and artists.
> After ten years of co-inspiration Michael has become a dear friend and confidant. Testament to his unfailing commitment to being a good person as much as to his art. ”

—
[MATTHEW RICE & MATTHIAS ROUX - CASIMI GUITARS](https://casimiguitars.com/)

> “I recently had the pleasure of hosting Michael Watts at my workshop, and I can't praise him highly enough. Not only is he an incredibly talented musician who brought out the best in my guitars, but he's also a true professional with a keen eye for detail and a passion for storytelling.
>
> Michael's video and audio recordings beautifully captured the nuances and unique character of each instrument. His ability to showcase the subtle details of my craftsmanship, combined with his warm and engaging personality, truly made my guitars shine.
>
> Beyond his musical and technical skills, Michael is a joy to work with. He's communicative, reliable, and brings a positive energy to every project. I was thoroughly impressed with his professionalism, creativity, and dedication to delivering exceptional results.
>
> If you're looking for a skilled musician, videographer, and storyteller to showcase your instruments, look no further than Michael Watts. He's simply the best!
> ”

—
[GIULIANO NICOLETTI - IULIUS GUITARS](https://www.iuliusguitars.com/)

> “'For more than a decade I have had the joy of Michael Watts’s unbiased guidance and belief in my work. As a builder who simply loves working with wood, the promotion of the work I do does not come first or naturally. Michael has been a huge part of sharing the work I make out to the people that also care. Whether through videos about making, chatting about what’s happening in the workshop or sharing photos and projects, a more supportive ambassador you will not find. Michael has been, and continues to be, an instrumental part of Taran Guitars and everything that it holds for the future.'”

—
[RORY DOWLING - TARAN GUITARS](https://www.taranguitars.co.uk/)

> “We have been working with Michael for years and hope many more to come! I can rely on Michael to beautifully demonstrate and record our instruments to the highest level. This is extremely valuable to my business, because it helps potential customers evaluate a range of our instruments intimately, keeping a key variable constant. Many customers who I’m not able to meet in person tell me they’ve found these demos and videos extremely helpful. Having access to an artist of this talent, reliability and friendliness is a real asset! Michael is a steadfast presence and a valuable communicator within our niche industry. He has great reach and has created an online audience that perfectly meets my target customer too.”

—
[ROSIE HEYDENRYCH - TURNSTONE GUITARS](https://www.turnstoneguitar.co.uk/)

![](https://images.zoogletools.com/s:bzglfiles/u/9846/4843e393e7c1952a365d08171ef780769dd69eb2/original/screenshot-2022-01-19-at-17-06-37.png/!!/b%3AW1sicmVzaXplIiwxMDAwXSxbIm1heCJdLFsid2UiXV0%3D/meta%3AeyJzcmNCdWNrZXQiOiJiemdsZmlsZXMifQ%3D%3D.png)

## INSTRUMENT SHOWCASE

![](https://images.zoogletools.com/s:bzglfiles/u/9846/d6fce4ff60dd7f5ff8f88c2b918c4959cb40977b/original/img-5855.jpg/!!/b%3AW1siZXh0cmFjdCIseyJsZWZ0IjoyMSwidG9wIjoyNzEsIndpZHRoIjo1ODA3LCJoZWlnaHQiOjMzNzV9XSxbInJlc2l6ZSIsMTAwMF0sWyJtYXgiXSxbIndlIl1d/meta%3AeyJzcmNCdWNrZXQiOiJiemdsZmlsZXMifQ%3D%3D.jpg)

web.title: Hello from Onwards Guitar House! As many of you know ... - Instagram
web.description: At The Guitar Shop, we pride ourselves on providing expert guitar repair services to musicians in Rosemount, MN. Our skilled technicians are ...
